coredata.dc:description: Solid-solid interfaces play a crucial role in fundamental aspects of electron transfer and ion transport, which determine the underlying physical phenomena and electrochemical behaviors in solid-state batteries. Nevertheless, the exact mechanisms at the solid-state battery interface have not been fully elucidated, which necessitates the localized probes to comprehensively understand their physical contact, interphase formation, interfacial instability, and mechanical properties. In this review, a variety of emerging imaging techniques to understand the local structure and chemistry at solid-state battery interfaces are overviewed, with special focus on how each imaging technique can address the key challenges at cathode-electrolyte interfaces, anode-electrolyte interfaces, and interparticle interfaces from the atomic scale to the macroscopic scale. The capabilities and limitations of these imaging methods are summarized and discussed. We also provide a vision of the state-of-the-art imaging characterization techniques and highlight new operando capabilities from cryogenic or synchrotron imaging methods to track the dynamic solid-state interface behaviors.

head.ce:title: Multi-scale Imaging of Solid-State Battery Interfaces: From Atomic Scale to Macroscopic Scale

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-solid interfaces play a crucial role in fundamental aspects of electron transfer and ion transport, which determine the underlying physical phenomena and electrochemical behaviors in solid-state batteries. Nevertheless, the exact mechanisms at the solid-state battery interface have not been fully elucidated, which necessitates the localized probes to comprehensively understand their physical contact, interphase formation, interfacial instability, and mechanical properties. In this review, a variety of emerging imaging techniques to understand the local structure and chemistry at solid-state battery interfaces are overviewed, with special focus on how each imaging technique can address the key challenges at cathode-electrolyte interfaces, anode-electrolyte interfaces, and interparticle interfaces from the atomic scale to the macroscopic scale. The capabilities and limitations of these imaging methods are summarized and discussed. We also provide a vision of the state-of-the-art imaging characterization techniques and highlight new operando capabilities from cryogenic or synchrotron imaging methods to track the dynamic solid-state interface behaviors.

The recent discovery of solid-state electrolytes with high conductivity has given rise to tremendous advances in the development of solid-state batteries. However, they still face the barrier of interfacial issues that restrict their future applications. Efforts to direct observation and analysis of solid-state battery interfaces are still lacking. Recent review articles have examined various solid-state batteries based on traditional chemical and physics insights, such as conductivity and stability. Recognizing this, here, we seek to evaluate a variety of emerging imaging techniques to understand the local interfacial structure and chemistry, with special focus on how each technique can address the key challenges at multiple interfaces from atomic scale to macroscopic scale. We assess the state-of-the-art imaging techniques and highlight new operando capabilities from cryogenic or synchrotron imaging methods to track the dynamic interface behaviors.

Emerging multi-scale imaging techniques with high spatial, temporal, and chemical resolution provides unique tools to elucidate the underlying mechanisms in battery electrochemical reactions. Here, the recent significant progress in a number of rapidly growing imaging techniques that have been applied to solid-state battery interfaces is summarized. The basic principle and experimental setup of these imaging methods are briefly introduced. The unique capabilities of each technique and the scientific insights in a variety of solid-state battery interfaces are highlighted.

ce:section.ce:para:
In the past decade, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have gained tremendous interest owing to their high energy density for practical applications in portable electronics and electric vehicle (EV) industry. Especially, paired with lithium (Li)-metal anodes that show the lowest electrochemical potential and highest specific capacity, Li-metal batteries have been deemed as the “holy grail” of electrochemical energy storage. Nevertheless, in terms of flammability and low thermal stability, conventional Li-metal batteries with liquid organic electrolytes cannot meet the requirements of practical application due to these safety concerns. 1 Solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) are considered safer due to their non-volatile and non-flammable natures. Replacing liquid electrolytes with SSEs, all-solid-state Li batteries (ASSLBs) therefore holds great promise in delivering high energy density with improved safety. 2
,
3 However, ASSLBs that can run stably with high energy density and long lifetime have not been achieved. The key challenge for ASSLBs lies in the low ionic conductivity of the SSEs and the large interfacial impedances of SSE-electrode interfaces. In particular, the high interfacial impedance and low interface stability of SSEs have severely hindered the electrochemical performances of ASSLBs. 4

Different from solid-liquid interfaces in a conventional Li battery, interfaces in ASSLBs involve many complex processes and chemical reactions, including interfacial wettability, physical contact, chemical or electrochemical reaction, ionic transport, space-charge layer, etc. 5–7 A fundamental understanding of the microstructure and chemical composition of the interfaces between SSEs and electrode is of paramount importance for the rational design of the high-performance solid-state battery, which necessitates advanced characterization methods. Nevertheless, the characterization of the physical and chemical properties of the solid-state battery interfaces are critically challenging because these interfaces are rigid and difficult to be penetrated by most conventional characterization tools, and the intact interfaces are easy to be damaged when disassembled. Despite these challenges, researchers have developed some advanced techniques to probe the structural, compositional, and morphological variations occurring at the electrode-SSE interfaces that are associated with the interfacial dynamics behavior and the solid-state battery performance. Conventional characterization techniques are generally helpful but are insufficient to reveal the fundamental mechanisms in solid-state batteries, which arouse great interest in direct visualization of the variation of the interfacial process. Therefore, the development of advanced spatially resolved characterizations with chemical or structural analysis is expected to provide unprecedented insight into the underlying interfacial behaviors and degradation mechanisms more accurately and reliably.

In this review, we first present a summary of a variety of interface issues and challenges that encompass the fundamental interface-property relationships in solid-state batteries with a focus on the interfacial transport, and try to describe the different transport behaviors of ions and electrons at both the Li-metal anode and cathode sides. The second part of this review illustrates recent advances in imaging characterization techniques from the atomic scale to the macroscopic scale and attempts to probe solid-state batteries under multiscales. In particular, we highlight how these imaging methods can elucidate the complex processes in anode-electrolyte interfaces, cathode-electrode interfaces, and SSE plates. By elucidating the intricate relations between interfacial behaviors and electrochemical performance, new mechanistic insights into solid-state battery interfaces can be anticipated to provide strategies for the design of high-performance solid-state batteries. Future perspectives on emerging imaging methods, in particular, synchrotron X-ray microscopy and cryo-transmission electron microscopy under in situ or in operando conditions, are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-State Battery Interfaces and Their Complexities
ce:section.ce:para: A prominent drawback of solid-state batteries is the heavy reliance of ionic diffusion on close contact of solid particles. The solid-solid interfaces in solid-state batteries are typically incomplete due to the hard contact between electrodes and electrolytes, as opposed to solid-liquid interfaces in conventional batteries in which liquid electrolytes with high mobility contributes to a conformal interface layer for efficient ionic migration. Therefore, redox reactions with active materials and electrons can only take place at the contacted electrolyte-electrode interface, driving the Li ions to diffuse through their interconnected region. Inspired by that, keeping an effective solid-solid electrolyte-electrode interface all the time in batteries is especially important to ensure that the charge-transfer reaction is stable. However, some key interface challenges from the insights of physics and chemistries in a solid-state battery include (1) insufficient physical contact and poor wettability; (2) large interfacial resistance and poor ionic transport; (3) poor electrochemical compatibility with electrodes, such as Li-metal anodes and high-voltage cathode materials; (4) space-charge layer; and (5) dendrite issues.

Physical contact and wettability are imperative in developing high-performance solid-state batteries. 8 Unlike liquid electrolytes that usually show good wettability with electrodes due to the fluidity and permeability, 9 a solid-state electrolyte is nonflowing and the physical contact in a solid-state battery obeys the point-to-point model. These point contacts are especially severe in the brittle and rigid solid-state oxide electrolyte with much higher Young’s modulus. The decomposition of SSEs and chemomechanical contraction of battery particles during the initial cycles also lead to interfacial physical contact loss. Moreover, this issue may get worse during long-term cycling in which the volume change and developed stress of battery-active particles can aggravate the propagation of cracks and delamination of solid-solid interfaces. Regarding the interfacial bonding mechanism, it is considered that the interfacial wettability is fundamentally determined by the surface energy mismatch between SSEs and electrodes. However, the interface degradation during electrochemical processes has been investigated only by conventional electrochemical measurement methods and has not been directly linked to morphological or chemical evolution at the interfaces. Understanding the underlying mechanism that governs the physical or chemical process at interfaces is necessary to make solid-state batteries a practical candidate for next-generation energy storage devices.

Interfacial ion transport is crucial for understanding solid-state electrochemical reactions. 10 The solid-solid interfaces are inherently highly resistive, which inhibit electron and ion transport during the charge and discharge process, significantly governing the electrochemical reaction kinetics in a solid-state battery. The ionic transport mechanism is complex and varies with the form of SSEs. For crystalline SSEs, ion migration is realized by their defect sites and properties, such as the ion concentration and distribution. In contrast, in a glassy SSE, ion transport is generally achieved by exciting ions moving from the local site to the neighboring site. 11 These differences significantly affect Li-ion diffusion at solid-solid interfaces. A clear understanding of its roles and elaborate fabrication of solid-solid interfaces are very helpful to achieve efficient solid-state batteries, but the issues of electronic and ionic transports at the interfaces on both Li anode and cathode sides are extremely complicated. Advanced characterization methods and modeling strategies are required to boost the fundamental understanding of buried interfaces in solid-state interfaces.

In addition to poor interfacial ion transport, instability of the electrode-electrolyte interface has been a serious concern for solid-state batteries. 12
,
13 Upon contact of the SSE with electrodes, a passivated interphase is often generated by the chemical or/and electrochemical side reactions at the interfaces, which hinders Li+ diffusion across the electrode-SSE interfaces. At the anode interface, the alkali metal anode can reduce many metal-containing solid electrolytes to produce resistive interlayers. Only a few SSEs show good stability against the highly active alkali metal anode. The thermodynamically unstable, mixed-conducting interphases can facilitate the continuous degradation of SSEs and increase the interfacial resistance. Compositional tailoring of SSEs or building kinetically stable interphases with the ability to conduct Li ions and blocking further interfacial reaction could be a solution. At the cathode interfaces, some SSEs may undergo oxidative degradation upon high-voltage cycling because of their intrinsically narrow electrochemical stability window. Extending the electrochemical stability window of SSEs via composition regulation or artificial interphase layer modification is a good strategy. Besides, element mutual diffusion may also take place at the cathode-electrode interfaces. First-principles calculations from Mo et al. indicate that cathode interfacial reactions could result in transition-metal migration from the cathode-active material across the electrode-electrolyte interfaces, 14
,
15 deteriorating interface stability upon cycling. Moreover, the electrode fabrication process, in particular, for solid-state oxide electrolytes with rigid interfaces, often involves heat treatment under high temperatures to improve the interface adhesion and wettability. Nevertheless, the high temperatures could also accelerate transition element mutual diffusion across the interfaces, leading to high interfacial transfer resistances. Introducing additional interphase may suppress the undesirable interfacial reactions by mitigating direct contact between SSE and the cathode electrolyte, but long-term interfacial stability requires more fundamental studies.

Dendrite formation is another serious issue in solid-state batteries. 16 With the inhomogeneous deposition of alkali metal as a fragment-like structure, the dendrite may grow even much easier in SSEs than in liquid-based electrolytes. Unfortunately, till now, few effective approaches have been employed to suppress the Li-dendrite growth ultimately, and the exact mechanism for the “unexpected” dendrite formation remains uncertain. It was thought that dendrites can form at a lower critical current density (0.9–4 mA cm−2) in SSEs than that of the liquid electrolyte (4–10 mA cm−2), which may be attributed to the high local current density created by the rough solid-solid interface with surface flaws, physical contact loss, and poor wettability. 17 Even worse, the dendrite growth in SSE pellet was recently reported. By penetrating through grain boundaries of solid electrolytes, Li ions can be reduced to metallic Li through electron transfer in the garnet electrolyte pellet. Extensive efforts to comprehensively elucidate the fundamental mechanism of dendrite growth through solid electrolytes are underway.

The space-charge layer was initially considered as a natural bridge between the bulk properties and the neighboring phase, accounting for the conductivity in a heterogeneous system. 18 In recent years, the space-charge layer effect was extended to qualitatively explain the local Li-ion-deficient region in the SSE near the cathode, which results from the extraction of Li ions from the electrolyte to the cathode side, driven by the potential difference between electrolyte and cathode. Also, this interfacial Li-ion depletion can further persistently develop during the charging process, which is evident in the disappearance of the potential slope at the initial stage of charging in the voltage profile as the thickness of the buffer layers increases. The generation of the space-charge layer strongly limits the Li-ion conductivity of electrolytes and thereby the rate capability of solid-state batteries. However, direct measurement of such a space-charge layer is still challenging owing to the complication of interfacial defect chemistry near the two-phase boundary and the technological difficulties of atomic-scale characterization.

Even worse, a stubborn challenge for the solid-state interface investigation is that the intact interfaces are embedded in SSBs, making it difficult to be isolated without damaging. A cross-section image of the electrode-electrolyte interface provides more convincing results, but specific techniques for sample preparation are required. If a more effective way, such as focus ion beam, could be developed to cut the cross-section of the sample with precision, many conventional methods such as cross-section scanning electron microscopy (SEM), slice transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersed X-ray analysis (EDX), and electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS) could be utilized to elucidate the nature of the interface. Further, given the in situ investigations, the rational design of battery configurations to expose the interfaces at the micro- and nanoscale level should be considered as a preferable solution. Especially, some air-sensitive samples like sulfides should be encapsulated in the inert gas environment, which can be Kapton film, epoxy resin, glass plates, etc. Besides, developing high-intensitive signal sources with high transmission are nondestructive characterization techniques to avoid the SSE absorption and therefore improve the resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-scale Imaging Methods from Atomic Probes to Macroscopic Probes
ce:section.ce:para: The interfacial properties at the electrolyte-electrode determine the electrochemical performance of a solid-state battery. Understanding the underlying phenomena that govern the interface ion transport and electrochemical kinetics is crucial to build a high-performance solid-state battery. Advanced characterization of electrode-electrolyte interfaces with high spatial resolution can accurately reveal the underlying solid-state phenomena but remain challenging owing to the high chemical reactivity of alkali metal anode and the condensed matter properties of solid-state interfaces. Li-ion transport and electrochemical reaction in a solid-state battery involve a multi-scale process from an atomic scale to the macroscopic scale and above. A combination of different imaging tools is often necessary to comprehensively probe the true solid-state interfacial behaviors. In this section, some imaging methods that have been employed in monitoring solid-state battery interfaces are briefly overviewed, based on their spatial resolution, structural analysis, dimensionality and so on, as summarized in Figure 1 .

Observing the solid-state interface process at the atomic scale is a long-standing dream for researchers. Considering the thickness and local behaviors at a solid-state interface, advanced imaging tools with ultra-high resolution down to the atomic scale provide insights into interfacial dynamics for solid-state batteries.

The Li-ion migration in a solid-state battery is a function of interconnected sites, as defined by the arrangement of anions in SSEs and the interfaces. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technique is an ideal method to probe the small changes at local structural information that are directly related to a solid-state battery, including defect sites, Li-ion transport, and the changes of electronic structure and valence of the cations that are involved in the electrochemical reaction processes. 19
,
20 Furthermore, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can directly map the distribution of chemical species, especially Li distribution at interfaces and in the bulk of SSEs to establish a correlation between Li deficiency and interfacial impedance, which is extremely crucial for quantifying Li diffusion in solid-state battery interfaces. Typically, Romanenko and co-workers reported the first quantitative in situ MRI study of the Li density distribution in a solid-state battery with high sensitivity. 21 Chien et al. employed three-dimensional 7Li MRI to reveal the increased heterogeneity of Li distribution upon electrochemical cycling. 22

High-resolution electron microscopy techniques (SEM, HRTEM) enable us to image the structure of a matter at the atomic or nanoscale. Coupling with an EDX or an EELS analysis, it can directly characterize the chemical, morphological, and structural information of a sample. Given that light compositions, such as Li metal or SSE interphase are unstable under the electron beam, the cryo-TEM technique previously used in structural biology has been used in battery studies recently. 23
,
24 Cryogenic circumstance not only helps to minimize beam damage issues but also preserves the intrinsic structural information of the sample. Combining EELS techniques, cryo-TEM can provide new insight into the correlation between the Li-metal dendrite formation mechanism and SSE interface. 25–27

In addition to NMR and high-resolution electron microscopy, some other imaging tools can also visualize morphological or structural information with a spatial resolution down to the atomic and nanoscale. Such analyses, corroborated by NMR, HRTEM, and vibrational spectroscopies, can serve to establish the correlation between solid-state interfaces and ionic transport kinetics, and ultimately address the key challenges in all-solid-state batteries.

Taking the advantages of high flux and energy tunability, synchrotron X-ray imaging provides a unique and nondestructive approach that allows researchers to observe solid-state battery interfaces at a broad range from a large scale (up to millimeter) to a small scale (down to nano), and the spatial resolution of synchrotron X-ray imaging techniques can be defined by the different imaging models. 28
,
29 Under the absorption mode, the directly transmitted X-rays project an image of the sample onto the detector, and the resulting absorption contrast image is the spatial distribution of the linear attenuation coefficient within the sample. 30
,
31 The resolution ranges from micro to sub-micro, which is determined by the achievable beam sizes, limitation in focusing optics, and the available detectors. Using Fresnel zone plates made with a robust nanofabrication process and a charge-coupled device (CCD), a 10 nm spatial resolution can be achieved with synchrotron X-ray absorption microscopy. Owing to the high coherence of the new-generation synchrotron source, newly developed lensless X-ray diffraction microscopy has offered new capabilities for imaging matter with sub-atomic resolution. Significant advances in synchrotron X-ray imaging methodologies have allowed absorption, phase contrast, Bragg coherent diffractive, and ptychographic and fluorescence imaging capabilities, offering spatially resolved compositional and structural information at the micro- and mesoscopic scales. In terms of X-ray energy, synchrotron X-ray imaging methods can be divided into two categories, soft X-ray and hard X-ray. 32
,
33 Soft X-ray at low energies usually work on thin samples and enable imaging of light elements, including samples with low absorption. Hard X-ray at high energies can penetrate thick samples, which is appropriate for solid-state battery study. In addition to 2D imaging, a unique capability for synchrotron X-ray imaging is 3D imaging, i.e., tomography that has the advantage of being nondestructive than other 3D characterizations, such as focused-ion beam (FIB) SEM tomography. Furthermore, taking advantage of the high X-ray energy, hard X-ray imaging is an excellent probe for in operando study of the solid-state battery’s electrochemistry during charging and discharging.

In addition to that, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is a supplementary means for effective interface analysis, which can provide locally chemical information to reveal the element distribution in the interface region. Together with the argon-ion etching technology, the space-resolution elemental valence state can be verified to monitor and understand the true interface reactions and evolutions. Further, the synchrotron-based XPS can lead a much wider probing-depth range that is also more sensitive than traditional XPS arising from the high-quality X-ray sources. Besides, atomic force microscopy (AFM) can provide microstructure imaging mapping, and therefore has attracted much attention in the battery interface study, most typically as solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) layer in the liquid electrolyte batteries. However, the application of AFM in the solid-state battery interface is limited due to the complexity of the solid-solid interface, which is easily broken in the sample preparation process. Considering the high sensitivity of AFM characteristics, the specially designed model battery may be an ideal object.

The neutron beam intrinsically carries no electric charge and can more easily penetrate through samples than X-rays with great thickness. Therefore, neutron-derived techniques are ideal tools to detect samples with macroscopic scale and above. Also, the technical features enable a much larger volume of a sample, such as a whole battery cell to be probed and more complex sample environments to be employed. Neutron-depth profiling (NDP) technique also presents high sensitivity to light elements with a low atomic number, (Li, B, and Na), making it a powerful tool to investigate the electrochemical reactions in solid-state batteries. 34 Especially, NDP owns characteristics of quantitative analysis allowing for a fundamental understanding of Li-ion transport at solid-solid interfaces and the failure modes of ASSLBs. 35 In addition to neutron microscopy, optical microscopy can also be used for LIB research due to its convenience and universality. 36 The main restriction of optical microscopy is its low spatial resolution, but it is generally sufficient to observe some large-sized samples, such as Li dendrites at the metallic anode sides of the entire electrode. Also, this deficiency can be compensated for by combining it with other ex situ tools, such as electron microscopy.
Here, the comparison of main technical parameters and the characteristics of advanced imaging characterizations in solid-state Li batteries are summarized in the Table 1 .

At the anode-electrolyte interface, inhomogeneous Li deposition and large interfacial resistance caused by volume changes of the electrodes upon cycling are also deemed as a serious problem in an all-solid-state battery. However, a deep study on the formation and evolution of Li dendrite in SSB chemistries remains challenging. To unveil the underlying mechanism behind dendrite chemistries, various characterization techniques are expected to investigate fundamental aspects of metallic Li anodes. These tools can generally be classified into two categories: those employed to investigate the macrostructural Li deposition and those employed to study the microstructural surface chemistry. Characterization techniques that meet these two requirements include MRI, NDP imaging, optical microscopy, X-ray computed tomography (CT), SEM, TEM, and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) with EELS for sub-micro or even nanoscale structural investigations. 29

In a solid-state battery, the potential failure of the SSBs is closely related to the chemical and electrochemical reactions at the anode interface, illustrated in Figure 2 A, which shows the Li dendrites growing along with the surface flaws and grain boundaries (red). 47 This prompted us to probe and understand the origins of dendrite formation and growth. 7Li MRI, a bulk phase characterization technique, can be used to observe the heterogeneous changes of the SSE surface caused by dendrite growth during electrochemical cycling (Figure 2B). 22 To further probe the buried dendrite and interface, the neutron-based technique is a good alternative. NDP imaging is especially effective to investigate light elements, such as Li dendrite, in a nondestructive manner, from which it can be deduced that the accumulation of the deposited Li metal within the localized SSEs under high current densities lead to poor stripping reversibility and then eventual battery failure (Figure 2C). 48 In addition, photomicroscopy also offers a conventional morphology characterization method to observe the Li dendrite formation at anode interfaces with different SSEs (LPS, β-Li3PS4, polycrystalline, and single-crystal LLZTO). 49
,
50

The most recent advances in nano-CT (Figure 2D) have shown a spatial resolution below 50 nm probing the Li dendrite at 3D scale, 51 which also has been proven useful when investigating buried parasitic Li-metal depositions in pouch cells in a noninvasive manner (Figure 2E). 52 With X-ray CT reconstruction, a series of questions that might be answered, such as how and where Li-metal deposits during fast charging, what the potential relationship between electrode structure and metallic Li deposition is, and how the deposited Li evolves upon repeated cycling. One limitation in nano-CT technique is the poor X-ray absorption of a light element (Li), which makes it sometimes hard to differentiate Li dendrite from the interior existed voids within the SSEs, but some operation strategies, such as synchrotron white X-ray beam, phase contrast, or de-confusing mode may be helpful for achieving better image contrast of Li dendrite. To obtain a high resolution, electron microscopy has been employed to investigate the heterogeneous Li deposition (Figure 2F). 53 The physical morphology and elemental distribution of dendrite can be obtained when SEM is combined with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analyzers. To further explore the refined structure formed at anode-electrolyte interface (Figure 2G), TEM can visualize the evolution of Li dendrite and the interfacial physical/chemical behaviors with a nanoscale resolution. 50,54–56 With the combination of the EELS technique, STEM can offer unambiguously rich information from bulk phase (dendrite morphology) down to the atomic scale (dendrite microstructure) to serve for interfacial nanoengineering. 57

Based on the imaging results, it was found that the formation mechanism and component of Li dendrites at the anode interfaces with different electrolytes show some differences. Due to the high flexibility, the Li-polymer interfaces show relatively close contact, and therefore, the growth mechanism of Li dendrites is similar to that in the liquid electrolyte-based Li batteries. 58 It is even interesting that the component of Li dendrites was confirmed as LixCy, Li2O, and LixCyOz recently, 59 which present hollow tubes but not the traditional rod-like morphology. However, the formation and growth of Li dendrites at anode interfaces with inorganic electrolytes are usually related to uneven current density from point-to-point physical contact. Here, the interfacial current distribution significantly affects the Li deposition behavior, which is further accelerated by the persistent formation of a passive layer from side reactions in the long-term cycles. Moreover, the Li dendrites usually grow along the grain boundaries driven by spontaneous dynamics.

Overall, the localized interface behavior at the anode-electrolyte interface poses a huge challenge to unravel the relationship between dendrite formation and the surrounding environment. Although the diverse characterization techniques have been developed to study the anode-electrolyte interfaces, a comprehensive understanding of Li nucleation, stripping, propagation, and interfacial chemistry is still highly needed. Considering that Li-electrolyte interfaces are electron-sensitive under many characterization conditions, the development of advanced imaging characterization technology is still crucial to inspire novel ideas and possibilities in solid-state battery research.

As the key component, developing SSEs with high bulk ionic conductivity and fast interfacial ion transport is crucial for the practical application of solid-state batteries. Intensive research efforts have led to the study of the key bottleneck of poor ion conductivity, which requires the capability to distinguish the local Li-ion mobility from the long-range Li-ion-transport process. NMR has the unique ability to probe the spontaneous Li-ion exchange between different phases, giving high selectivity for charge transfer over phase boundaries in SSEs. 60 Recently, 2D Li-ion exchange NMR has been employed to access the spontaneous ion transport over the interface between an argyrodite solid electrolyte and a sulfide electrode, providing new insight into interfacial ion transport for solid-state batteries (Figure 3 A). 61 More recently, a 3D 7Li MRI study was also reported to observe Li distribution heterogeneity in solid-state Li10GeP2S12 electrolyte battery. The 7Li MRI data and the derived map revealed a significant Li loss at solid-solid interfaces upon electrochemical cycling, which established the direct correlation between Li deficiency and interfacial resistance (Figure 3B). 22

In addition to a single-component SSE, exploring novel SSEs, hybrid solid electrolytes, are expected to integrate the merits of individual electrolytes to achieve fast ion conduction and high stability simultaneously for solid-state batteries. Fundamental experimental study of the effect of the ratio and integration of individual electrolytes on ion-transport behavior is therefore important for resolving the debate mechanism about Li-ion pathways in hybrid SSEs. Most typically as the polymer-ceramic composite electrolytes, it was demonstrated that an amorphous polymer shell can form around filler particles that contribute to the increased ionic conductivity. However, some studies deduced that Li ions diffused through the ceramic particles play dominant effects. Experimental evidence is highly needed to resolve the confusion surrounding Li-ion pathways in hybrid electrolytes. Nevertheless, Li is a very light element and difficult to characterize with electron- or X-ray-based techniques. By combining selective isotope labeling and solid-state Li NMR, Zheng etal. tracked Li-ion pathways within Li7La3Zr2O12 (LLZO)-polyethylene oxide (PEO) composite electrolytes by monitoring the replacement of 7Li in the hybrid electrolyte by 6Li from the 6Li metal electrodes upon electrochemical cycling, confirming that Li ions prefer to maximize the fraction of the LLZO ceramic phase and minimize that of the interface or the PEO polymer phase on their diffusion pathway. 62 Further, fast Li-ions transport in the hybrid electrolyte with different fillers was also determined with tracer-exchange Li NMR in PEO-LGPS, PEO-perovskite Li3/8Sr7/16Ta3/4Zr1/4O3, and PEO-Li+ insulating oxides. 63–65

Interfacial ion-transport properties is also important for a solid-state battery in addition to the intrinsic ion-transport behaviors, which are related to 3D electrode microstructure features, such as grain boundaries, grain orientation, pores, and density in the SSE pellet. The fundamental mechanisms between the properties of SSEs (elasticity, morphology, and structure) and solid-solid interfaces have not been well understood. Porosity (the percentage of void space to total volume) and tortuosity (a dimensionless quantity representing the twisting and winding property of a porous electrode and its effective transport properties) are often used to describe the effective ion-transport behavior in a 3D structure. With the accurate 3D reconstruction of an electrode, these fine microstructural parameters can be obtained experimentally to determine the occurrence of continuous ionic transport paths (Figures 4A and 4B). 66 The effect of pore connectivity on Li dendrite propagation within LLZO electrolytes can be investigated with synchrotron X-ray tomography. 67 By tracking Li penetration within the pores of SSEs, it was found that the formation of isolated Li agglomerates can mechanistically occur within LLZO plate, and this phenomenon can be amplified when pore regions are connected within the electrolyte, which necessitates the manufacturing methods to ensure high-density electrolytes and minimize the grain boundary resistance. Similarly, with a lab X-ray CT, 68 chemomechanical fracture processes and interphase growth in Li1+xAlxGe2–x(PO4)3 (LAGP) solid electrolytes can be directly visualized (Figure 4C). The growth of interphase with volume expansion drives large crack formation in an SSE, which plays a primary role in the increase of ion-transport impedance and electrochemomechanical degradation. In addition to the 3D structure in an electrolyte pellet, the mixture process and filler particle distribution in hybrid electrolytes also affect ionic transport and conductivity (Figure 4D). 69 In particular, particle aggregation often occurs at higher loadings, which may significantly affect the hybrid electrolyte properties. With the X-ray tomography method, 3D distribution of fillers in the hybrid composite can be directly visualized and offer a mechanistic understanding of the correlation between ion transport and 3D microstructure. 70

Utilizing SSEs to replace liquid electrolyte was previously considered as a promising approach to inhibit dendrite growth in LIBs, but increasing studies show Li dendrite can grow through a solid-electrolyte pellet. Dendrite formation and propagation in SSEs are microstructurally driven and surface morphology dependent. It is generally accepted that during the continuous Li plating, a solid electrolyte may be subjected to mechanical constraints from the surrounding deposited Li within surface flaws, which can generate high stresses to initiate large cracks in SSEs and then lead to Li dendrite growth. X-ray tomography can offer 3D microstructural information within a solid-state electrode pellet to elucidate the mechanical strain and dendrite growth during electrochemical cycling. 71 Also, by combining an atomic force microscope with an environmental transmission electron microscope, 72 individual Li whisker can be directly observed to understand how an elastoplastic Li whisker might penetrate an elastically stiff solid electrolyte, and highlight the importance of surface roughness of SSE to suppress Li dendrite growth. Despite these advances, dendrite growth mechanisms remain controversial and call for more fundamental studies with advanced characterization tools.

The interfacial chemical reaction between electrode and SSE, and the resulting high interfacial resistance are detrimental for solid-state battery performance, which can be visualized by the nanoscale imaging technique. Here, we would like to emphasize the evolution of morphology, structure, and chemical properties arising from the interface chemical reactions. Wang et al. performed in situ STEM-EELS observation of interfacial behaviors related to charge transfer and ion transport in all-solid-state batteries (Figure 5 A). 57 The nanoscale spectroscopic characterization revealed the formation of a disordered interfacial phase at the LiCoO2-LiPON interface that accumulates Li2O and Li2O2 species along with the formation of rocksalt CoO after charging process, which can be attributed to the deposited cathode elements and the oxygen evolution reaction at solid-solid interfaces. The unexpected interfacial chemical reaction leads to high ion-transport resistance and performance decay in solid-state batteries (Figure 5B). 73 With an aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope at an atomic scale, single-crystal LiCoO2 particles were found to become nanosized polycrystals connected by coherent twin boundaries and antiphase domain boundaries in LiCoO2|LLZO|Au solid-state batteries. The pathway of Li ions along the grain boundaries in solid-state battery cathode was also suggested with this method. Recently, by using phase-shifting electron holography and spatially resolved EELS, Nomura reported direct observation of the Li-ionic space-layer-charge layer formed at SSE interface, offering new opportunities to engineer solid-state battery interfaces at nanoscale resolution (Figure 5C). 74

Understanding the migration of chemical elements at the cathode-SSE interfaces is critical to develop highly stabley solid-state batteries. Here, we mainly focus on the cross-section elemental diffusion phenomenon from the imaging studies. The interfacial elemental interdiffusion can be characterized by the combination of FIB with HRTEM. By slicing the interface with FIB, the cross-sectional HRTEM images and the chemical information of the interfaces can be directly obtained. The diffusion of transition-metal elements across the LiCoO2-SSE interface can be probed at nanoscale resolution (Figure 5D). 75 A study about Li accumulation and the interdiffusion of elements was also found at cathode-electrolyte (LixCoO2-LiPON) interfaces with STEM-EELS, 76 which can be accounted for the irreversible capacity losses at solid-state batteries. A more recent study detected elemental interdiffusion at LiCoO2 (LCO)-(Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3) (LATP) interface, and found ALD coating Li3PO4 layer can effectively prevent Co diffusion into the SSE. 75 Similarly, without a Li2SiO3 buffer layer, the coexistence of P, S, and Co was detected at the LiCoO2 and Li2S-P2S5 interfacial layer with STEM-EELS technique, 77 indicating the mutual diffusion at the cathode interface and the important role of a buffer layer.

Time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) is a semiquantitative method to detect the local enrichments of some fragments at interfaces, and the depth profiles can be reconstructed in 3D to show the spatial fragment distribution. Park et al. exploited 3D elemental maps of the LiCoO2-LLZO interface at the sub-micron resolution, clearly showing that the annealing process at a high temperature can fuse LiCoO2 and LLZO to induce the elemental cross diffusion and new interfacial phase formation. These degraded interface characters may introduce high electrochemical resistance and deteriorate the cycle stability (Figure 6 A). 78 Similarly, combining local compositional information from ToF-SIMS and complementary XPS, the degradation mechanism in the LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2-Li6PS5Cl-composite cathode of an ASSLB was directly visualized and indicated that sulfates and phosphates play an important role in the formation of an SEI at cathode interface (Figure 6B). 79

In addition to interfacial chemical behaviors, physical contact loss is a common phenomenon that contributes to the solid-state battery performance. The reason could be attributed to the rigid nature of the SSE, anisotropic structural changes, volume expansion, and state-of-charge heterogeneities.

SEM can offer a simple method to observe the physical contact loss between the active material and solid electrolyte provoked by the mechanical deflation during electrochemical cycling (Figure 6C). 80 To observe the internal as well as the external interfaces at 3D view, X-ray CT enables high-resolution visualization to identify the underlying mechanism of the electromechanical deformation at cathode-SSE interfaces. For example, an in situ X-ray nanotomography study of the FeS2 electrode reveals an anisotropically electrochemical-mechanical evolution in solid-state batteries, 81 providing new insights into the mechanism behind the solid-solid interfacial reaction. Meanwhile, with 3D geometrical properties and morphological changes directly represented with X-ray tomography, it can even map Li-ion concentration distribution and inhomogeneous composition in solid-state battery electrode. 69

The persistent pursuit of next-generation energy storage technologies with increasing energy density and safety requirements will compel an inevitable transfer from conventional LIBs to SSBs. However, solid-solid interfaces involve physical, chemical, and mechanical processes, which are considered to be the key determinants for performance and stability of solid-state battery. Directly observing the complex interfacial process with advanced imaging methods can provide an opportunity for solid-state interface electrochemistry. In this review, we highlighted the state-of-the-art imaging characterization techniques to shed light on the interface issues and challenges from nanoscale phenomena to macro and above scale within buried solid-solid interfaces in the solid-state batteries. In particular, the characterization techniques for Li dendrites at anode interfaces and electrolyte oxidations at cathode sides were mainly focused and discussed. Continuous achievements described above demonstrate the importance of imaging techniques to offer new insights into the poor kinetic and thermodynamic stability at the electrolyte-electrode interfaces. The capabilities, limitations, and application areas of various imaging technologies are summarized in Table 2 . Although meaningful progress has been made in interface research for solid-state batteries, we believe that there are great advances still awaiting to be discovered. Further research at interfaces of solid-state battery should pay more attention to a variety of imaging technologies and take full advantage of each technology to discover the interfacial mechanism from atomic to macro scale. Here, we outline some potential directions for future investigation at solid interface, which may result in pathways for valid practical solutions.

In the atomic and nanoscale, the developing cryo-EM opens a new way to revisit the previous issues in solid-state batteries and offers new insights into the interfacial kinetics. Operando cryo-EM experiments are highly expected to probe the dynamic dendrite growth at the anodic interface and interfacial reaction on the cathode sides to uncover the underlying interfacial mechanisms. Furthermore, it is a future trend to push the frontiers of current in situ characterization techniques for the more comprehensive investigation of interface fundamentals in solid-state batteries. Improvements in analytical techniques would allow tracing the time-resolved physical, chemical, and electrochemical reactions on the nanoscale and sub-nanoscale among relevant systems and interfaces.

In micro- and mesoscopic scales, considering high flux and high energy of synchrotron X-ray offer and the unique advantage of capability for operando studies, in situ synchrotron X-rays imaging is a sensitive and powerful tool that shows promise in the nondestructive probing of the atomic and mesoscale structures of solid-state batteries. In combination with diffraction and absorption analysis, finer spatial structural information can be extracted. Furthermore, the improvement of the resolution of single pixel from the current value of approximately 25 nm to a higher level of several nanometers would be a big breakthrough for fundamental research. We expect that the synchrotron-based X-ray imaging methods in multiple dimensions can achieve real-time visualization of Li plating and stripping under practical battery operation, providing true insights into morphology evolution during Li plating. Additionally, the X-ray imaging results can be handled by supplementary tools, such as artificial intelligence, deep learning, machine learning, etc., which offer an opportunity for imaging methods to open up a new era for solid-state batteries research.

In macroscale and above scale, optical microscopy can offer great convenience to diagnose the entire solid-state battery interfaces for the general purpose of troubleshooting, despite the limited spatial resolution. To obtain more comprehensive and in-depth interface information, one can locate some regions of interest at solid-state interfaces with a fast screening step. Combining with FIB and EDX, the enhanced system can provide abundant information containing surface morphological and component elements at solid-solid interfaces with multiple scales. Furthermore, HRTEM, assisted by FIB to obtain the sample slices under the direction of optical microscopy, can specifically help researchers to probe the local fine structure at the solid-state interfaces, tracking the atomic rearrangements at the interface and elucidate their effects on solid-state macroscale electrochemomechanical phenomenon. Also, other complementary techniques, including NMR, neutron, and ToF-SIMS, are necessary to visualize and quantify the interfacial physics and interfacial chemistry for advancing our understanding. A combination of the above imaging techniques spanning multiple length scales is always beneficial for exploring the complex interfaces in solid-state batteries.

Based on these advanced characterizing results, some target-guidance-interface-optimizing strategies should be considered to solve the interface issues in solid-state batteries. At the anode-electrolyte interfaces, specially designed artificial layers are highly encouraged to overcome the dendrite growth and high chemical reactivity of Li metal. The interfacial layer is preferably equipped with high interfacial lithiophilic ability, fast ion transport, high mechanical strength, and chemical and electrochemical stability. At the cathode/electrolyte sides, the rigid solid-solid contact, space-charge effects, and severe chemical instability at high working voltages should be paid more attention. Some solutions, including co-sintering of active materials and SSEs, buffer layer with high-voltage resistance, high flexibility and ion conductance, and electron-insulated characteristics are ideal candidates.

In summary, multitudinous imaging techniques may not be ultimate solutions for figuring out every issue and challenges faced by SSB interfaces. However, they are certainly becoming an enabler for a deeper understanding of interface phenomena from multiple scales and models, helping to better address solutions and design strategies for translating into improvements in materials and cell-level performance.
